risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A statistician from the University of California Davis, who was blinded to the intervention groups, generated a random allocation sequence at the level of the concession (extended family compound) using SAS V.9.3 ... to assign eligible children in the intervention cohort to one of four intervention groups." Comment: This explicitly states that a random allocation sequence was generated using SAS by a blinded statistician, which is a proper random method and is sufficient for Low risk.

Summary: The report states that a statistician generated the random allocation sequence and that participants and staff were blinded, but it does not explicitly describe any mechanism that would have prevented recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. The phrase "Every concession had a 1/8 chance of receiving one of the eight colour codes" describes assignment probability/blinding, not concealment from the enrolling person. Comment: Because no central allocation, SNOSE, sequentially numbered containers, or other explicit concealment method is reported, allocation concealment remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a community-based, double-blind, placebo-controlled, cluster-randomised trial" and "During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups." Comment: The study explicitly states that both participants and field staff/personnel were blinded, and the zinc/placebo products were made identical in appearance and flavour. Because the outcomes were morbidity outcomes assessed by caregiver report and active surveillance, successful blinding of participants and personnel makes performance bias unlikely.

Quote: "During the trial, all participants, field staff, study statistician and investigators were blinded to the intervention groups." Comment: Outcome assessment for morbidity was performed by trained field data collectors using structured weekly questionnaires, and these field staff were explicitly blinded. The main outcomes were largely symptom-based caregiver-reported morbidity (diarrhoea, fever, AURI, ALRI), but the assessors collecting and recording these outcomes were masked, reducing the chance that knowledge of allocation influenced measurement. The study also used some objective confirmation (auricular temperature, RDT, blood smear) for fever/malaria episodes, further limiting detection bias.

Quote: "Caregivers of 97% of the participating children provided information on at least 30 days of morbidity surveillance, and 78% of the caregivers provided information for at least 35 weeks. The 71 children who were excluded from the final analyses because they provided less than 30 days of morbidity observations did not differ at baseline from those included in the analysis (data not shown). There were no differences in participation or reporting rates among the four intervention groups." Comment: Attrition/missing data were low overall and appeared balanced across groups; exclusions were limited to children with very sparse follow-up, with no baseline differences reported and no signal of outcome-related or differential dropout, so risk of bias from incomplete outcome data is unlikely.
